unmapped_findings: The δ-ferrite phase exhibits a rotated cube texture component {100}<011> with maximum intensity of 2.57, but its effect on anisotropic strain hardening is deemed insignificant due to similar Taylor factors (2.83 for RD and 2.78 for TD).

Austenite yields first at engineering strains of ~1.8% (RD) and ~2.1% (TD), while ferrite yields at ~2.3% (RD) and ~2.9% (TD), as determined from lattice strain deviation from linearity in HEXRD data.
Strain-induced α'-martensite nucleation occurs primarily at γ/α and γ/δ phase boundaries due to high stress/strain concentrations and geometrically necessary dislocations at these interfaces.
The difference in martensite fraction between RD and TD samples reaches ~4 vol.% at a true strain of ~0.48.
Volume expansion associated with γ to α'-martensitic transformation (2~4%) contributes to local plastic strain in the γ-α' mixed phase.

metadata.title: On the anisotropic deformation behavior of a low-density medium manganese steel

process_sequence.others: Reduced to final thickness of ~1.5 mm
Produced ultrafine γ+α aggregate and δ-ferrite microstructure

structure.overall_structure: Banded/layered microstructure consisting of coarse δ-ferrite bands and ultrafine-grained γ+α aggregates aligned along the rolling direction

phases.coherence: Incoherent phase boundaries with high strain concentration

defect_interaction.interaction_type: Strain-induced martensite nucleation at phase boundaries
defect_interaction.quality_description: α'-martensite nucleates preferentially at γ/α and γ/δ phase boundaries due to high stress/strain concentrations and geometrically necessary dislocations accumulated at interfaces

others.strain_partitioning_RD: Iso-strain condition between δ-ferrite and γ+α aggregates when loaded parallel to banding direction

others.strain_partitioning_TD: Iso-stress condition with pronounced strain concentration in softer δ-ferrite when loaded perpendicular to banding direction

others.GND_accumulation: High density of geometrically necessary dislocations at phase boundaries to accommodate strain incompatibility between phases

interface.phase_evolution: Strain-induced transformation of γ-austenite to α'-martensite during plastic deformation, with faster kinetics in RD (rolling direction) compared to TD (transverse direction)

tensile_properties.stress_strain_curves: Engineering stress-strain curves show higher strength and ductility for RD compared to TD (Fig. 4a)

stress_strain_distribution.distribution: Uniform strain distribution between δ-ferrite and γ+α aggregates (iso-strain)
Heterogeneous strain with concentration in δ-ferrite; lower strain in austenite compared to RD

characterization_methods.purpose: Microstructure characterization, phase identification, grain size measurement, and texture analysis
characterization_methods.key_findings: Banded structure with δ-ferrite (44 vol.%, ~38 μm) and γ+α aggregate (56 vol.%, ~1.8-2 μm); FCC fraction 38.8%, BCC fraction 61.2%

characterization_methods.purpose: Deformation substructure analysis and dislocation observation
characterization_methods.key_findings: α'-martensite nucleation at γ/α and γ/δ phase boundaries; stacking faults in austenite; dislocation tangles in ferrite

characterization_methods.purpose: In-situ phase transformation, lattice strain, and dislocation density analysis during tensile testing
characterization_methods.key_findings: Faster martensite transformation kinetics in RD vs TD (25% vs 20% at true strain 0.48); higher dislocation density in austenite for RD (max ~1.51×10¹⁴ m⁻²); austenite yields before ferrite

characterization_methods.purpose: High-resolution strain partitioning analysis between phases
characterization_methods.key_findings: RD: iso-strain condition between δ and γ+α; TD: strain concentration in δ-ferrite (1.26× higher than γ+α); higher austenite strain in RD promotes TRIP effect

characterization_methods.purpose: Local hardness measurement of different microstructural regions

computational_details.simulation_method: Olson-Cohen model for martensite transformation kinetics; Thermo-Calc for phase diagram calculation
computational_details.software_used: Thermo-Calc (TCFE9 database), MAUD (Rietveld refinement), Fit2D (diffraction analysis), La Vision DaVis (DIC analysis), AZtecCrystal and TSL OIM (EBSD analysis)
computational_details.validation_against_experiment: Olson-Cohen model predictions match experimental martensite fraction data from HEXRD when accounting for strain partitioning factor of 1.15 between RD and TD

analytical_model.equation_role: Predict volume fraction of deformation-induced martensite as function of plastic strain
analytical_model.parameters: α=1.35, β=2, n=2, initial austenite fraction F_γ0=46.5 vol.%
analytical_model.model_note: Applied with strain correction factor ε_RD = 1.15 × ε_TD to account for strain partitioning differences between loading directions
